sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Abdominal pain is a very common symptom experienced by patients. In the majority of patients, the cause is benign and/or self-limiting. However, many causes can be time critical and may require urgent intervention.
- Time critical abdominal emergencies can include:
  - Ectopic pregnancy
  - Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurism (AAA)
  - Peritonitis and sepsis
  - Testicular/ovarian torsion
  - Uncontrolled GI bleeding
  - Bowel obstruction
  - Acute pancreatitis

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Severe abdominal pain and/or tenderness suggestive of peritonism
- Abdominal distention
- Constipation
- Altered Vital Signs, fever, diaphoresis, pallor, signs of sepsis
- Nausea and/or vomiting haematemesis, melena
- History of haematemesis and/or melaena or haematochezia
- Vaginal discharge/bleeding

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Cardiac pain can mimic gastro-oesophageal pain. Any patient with pain above the naval should get a 12 lead ECG, consent not withstanding
- The presence of normal vital signs does not preclude serious abdominal disease
- AAAs may masquerade as renal colic pain, especially in older patients

- Red Flags Requiring Immediate Attention
- Haemodynamic instability (shock, hypotension)
- Rigid abdomen (peritonitis)
- Severe, sudden-onset pain ("worst ever")
- Gastrointestinal bleeding (haematemesis, melena, haematochezia)
- Persistent vomiting or signs of bowel obstruction
- Positive pregnancy test with abdominal pain (risk of ectopic pregnancy)
- Known AAA with abdominal/back pain

**Conditions by region could be:**

**Right Hypochondriac:**
Gallstones or cholecystitis, hepatitis or liver abscess, duodenal ulcers, kidney stones or infection, trauma (#’s)

**Epigastric:**
Gastroesophageal reflux, gastritis, gastric ulcers, pancreatitis, cholecystitis, overeating

**Left Hypochondriac:**
Splenic enlargement or rupture, gastric ulcers, pancreatitis, kidney stones, trauma (#’s)

**Right Lumbar:**
Kidney stones or pyelonephritis, appendicitis (retrocecal), colon disorders (e.g., Crohn’s)

**Umbilical:**
Umbilical hernia, early appendicitis, gastroenteritis, small bowel disorders including obstruction or inflammation, AAA

**Left Lumbar:**
Kidney stones or pyelonephritis, diverticulitis, constipation or impaction

**Right Iliac Fossa:**
Appendicitis, Crohn’s disease, ovarian issues (e.g., cysts, torsions or ectopic pregnancy), hernias

**Hypogastric:**
Cystitis, pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), endometriosis, ectopic pregnancy, bladder distension

**Left Iliac Fossa:**
Diverticulitis, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), ovarian issues (e.g., cysts, torsions or ectopic pregnancy), hernia

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital signs
- Keep patient at rest and prevent movement, allow patient to find comfortable position
- Secondary Survey (including distal colour, temp, sensation, movement and pulses/cap refill)
- Abdominal auscultation, palpation, percussion
- Consider “special signs” (Murphy’s, McBurney’s, Rovsing, Psaos)
- Consider “over the counter” pain relief (Paracetamol, Ibuprofen)
- Urinalysis and pregnancy test where appropriate

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access
- Consider fluid resuscitation
- Consider appropriate analgesia
- 12 lead ECG

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- The target of fluid resuscitation in a dissecting or ruptured AAA, GI Bleed or ruptured ectopic pregnancy is to maintain perfusion to vital organs. Radial pulses can be used as a marker of perfusion
- Patients who have a dissecting AAA are usually in severe pain and require IV narcotic analgesia which should not be delayed or withheld
- Cardiac ischemia may present with abdominal pain, therefore all patients with abdominal pain should have a 12 lead ECG acquired and interpreted for risk stratification
- The assessment of abdominal pain is complex and requires multi-modal investigation
- Patient education: Explain warning signs that require emergency care (e.g., worsening pain, bleeding, fever). Advise follow up for further review and management
